english: A community hall in Berlin. A panel discussion about integration begins. Verumius sits in the audience, notebook ready. Learn the key words of German politics and democracy.
Learn 20 words from politics and democracy
Discover the world of the German panel discussion
Join Verumius in his first public debate

A community hall in Berlin. Rows of chairs filling up. A banner above the stage: 'Integration and Living Together.' Microphones on the panel table. Water glasses catching the fluorescent light. Verumius finds a seat, notebook on his knee.
A few rows ahead, Dr. Yilmaz turns and catches his eye. A subtle nod. 'You belong here.' No words needed.
A panel discussion in German. About politics. About integration. I understand everything. But formulating the question in my head, that is something else entirely.
The moderator steps to the microphone. 'Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to our debate. The topic is controversial, the viewpoints are different.'
the debate

A: How was the debate yesterday?
B: Very lively. Everyone had strong opinions.
A: Was there a clear debate?
B: Yes, the debate was fair and respectful.
democracy

A: What does democracy mean to you?
B: That the people decide.
A: Is democracy in danger?
B: No, but we must protect it.
the (political) party

A: Which party do you support?
B: I vote for whichever party convinces me.
A: Are there many parties?
B: Yes, six major parties in the Bundestag.
the politician (male)

A: Do you know the politician?
B: Yes, he is a member of the Bundestag.
A: Is he a good politician?
B: He represents his voters well.
the politician (female)

A: Who is this politician?
B: A well-known member of parliament.
A: What does the politician demand?
B: More equality in politics.
the government

A: What is the government doing about climate change?
B: The government has passed new laws.
A: Is the government popular?
B: The government has many critics.
the opposition

A: What does the opposition say?
B: The opposition criticizes the bill.
A: Is the opposition strong?
B: Yes, they have many members of parliament.
the parliament

A: How many members does the parliament have?
B: Over 700 in the Bundestag.
A: Does parliament meet every day?
B: No, parliament has session weeks.
the Bundestag (German federal parliament)

A: What is happening in the Bundestag today?
B: A vote on a new law.
A: Who sits in the Bundestag?
B: The elected members of all parties.
the viewpoint, the position

A: What is your viewpoint on this topic?
B: My position is clear: integration needs both sides.
A: Can you justify your viewpoint?
B: Of course. I support my position with facts.
the difference of opinion

A: Are there differences of opinion in the team?
B: Yes, but we discuss respectfully.
A: Is a difference of opinion bad?
B: No, it is normal and healthy.
political

A: Are you interested in political topics?
B: Yes, political education is important.
A: Is that a political problem?
B: Yes, it affects the whole society.
democratic

A: Is Germany a democratic country?
B: Yes, democratic elections are the foundation.
A: What are democratic values?
B: Freedom, equality, and human dignity.
parliamentary

A: What is a parliamentary question?
B: An official question to the government.
A: Must the government answer?
B: Yes, every parliamentary question is answered.
controversial

A: Is the topic controversial?
B: Yes, very controversial. Opinions differ.
A: Why is it so controversial?
B: Because it affects everyone.
contested, disputed

A: The proposal is contested.
B: Yes, many are against it.
A: Why is the decision so contested?
B: Because it does not convince everyone.
to criticize

A: The opposition criticizes the bill.
B: What exactly do they criticize?
A: They criticize the high costs.
B: Constructive criticism is important.
to convince

A: Did the speech convince you?
B: Not quite. The argument does not convince me.
A: How can one convince you?
B: With facts and good examples.
to support

A: Do you support this proposal?
B: Yes, I fully support it.
A: Who supports the reform?
B: The coalition supports it unanimously.
to defend

A: Are you defending your position?
B: Yes, I defend it with facts.
A: Can this position be defended?
B: Yes, it can be well defended.
A compound noun: a spatial metaphor for where someone stands intellectually. It describes a personal attitude or viewpoint in a discussion.
The group in parliament whose job is to scrutinize and challenge the government.
The opposition criticizes the government's bill.
He understands everything. But formulating a question publicly in German is a different challenge.

The moderator announces a break. Fifteen minutes. Verumius stays seated. He looks at his notes. Debate, democracy, viewpoint, difference of opinion. The words of a society that can argue without hating each other.
The language of parliament. Learn the key words for legislation, debates, and the structures of German democracy.
Learn 20 words from parliamentary language
Understand how German legislation works
Build your political vocabulary
The break is over. The audience returns to their seats. Verumius has his notebook open, the page half full. The vocabulary of democracy. He wants it all.
the counterargument

A: Do you have a counterargument?
B: Yes, my counterargument is simple.
A: Is the counterargument strong?
B: Yes, it completely refutes the thesis.
the coalition

A: Which parties form the coalition?
B: Three parties in the current coalition.
A: Is the coalition stable?
B: Yes, but compromises are necessary.
the compromise

A: Have the parties found a compromise?
B: Yes, after long negotiations.
A: Is a compromise weakness?
B: No, a compromise is political skill.
the parliamentary group

A: How large is the parliamentary group?
B: The parliamentary group has 200 members.
A: Does the parliamentary group always vote together?
B: Usually. Party discipline is real.
the bill, the draft law

A: Who wrote the bill?
B: The ministry drafted the bill.
A: Was the bill accepted?
B: No, the bill was rejected.
the committee

A: Which committee is responsible?
B: The Committee for Internal Affairs.
A: What does a committee do?
B: It examines laws and hears experts.
the member of parliament

A: Who is your member of parliament?
B: The member for my constituency.
A: What does a member of parliament do?
B: He represents the citizens in the Bundestag.
the reform

A: Do we need a reform?
B: Yes, the system needs a reform.
A: Which reform is most important?
B: The education reform, many say.
the election campaign

A: When does the election campaign begin?
B: The campaign starts three months before the election.
A: Is the campaign expensive?
B: Yes, but less than in America.
the Basic Law (German constitution)

A: What is in the Basic Law?
B: The fundamental rights of all citizens.
A: Can the Basic Law be changed?
B: Only with a two-thirds majority.
the fundamental right

A: What is a fundamental right?
B: For example, freedom of expression.
A: Can a fundamental right be abolished?
B: No, fundamental rights are inviolable.
the controversy

A: The controversy has lasted for weeks.
B: Yes, there is no simple solution.
A: Is the controversy justified?
B: Yes, both sides have good arguments.
the measure, the action taken

A: What measures is the government planning?
B: New measures against unemployment.
A: Are the measures sufficient?
B: No, we need more measures.
the concern, the reservation

A: Do you have concerns about the proposal?
B: Yes, I have serious concerns.
A: What concerns exactly?
B: My concerns relate to the costs.
the demand

A: What is the opposition's demand?
B: Their demand: lower taxes.
A: Is the demand realistic?
B: That depends on the compromise.
to reject, to decline

A: Did the parliamentary group reject the bill?
B: Yes, they rejected it unanimously.
A: Why do they reject it?
B: Because it is too expensive.
to decide, to pass (a law)

A: Did parliament pass the law?
B: Yes, it was passed yesterday.
A: Who made the decision?
B: The majority decided.
to object

A: What is your objection to it?
B: I object that the costs are too high.
A: Can one not object?
B: Of course, objections are always welcome.
to reply, to retort

A: What did the minister reply?
B: He replied: 'I see that differently.'
A: Was the response convincing?
B: Yes, he replied with strong facts.
to pass (a law), to adopt

A: Was the law passed?
B: Yes, it was passed last week.
A: How is a law passed?
B: By vote in the Bundestag.

A bill begins in a ministry or in the Bundestag.

1. First reading: introduction in the plenary session
2. Committee: detailed review, expert hearings, amendments
3. Second reading: committee report
4. Third reading: final vote
5. Bundesrat: approval by the states
6. Federal President: signature

Then: 'Verkündet im Bundesgesetzblatt.' Binding law.

Not every law needs Bundesrat approval. 'Zustimmungsgesetze' (consent laws) need it. 'Einspruchsgesetze' (objection laws) can override a Bundesrat veto with a Bundestag majority. The distinction matters because it determines how much power the Länder have over federal legislation.
The body that hears experts, works out amendments, and does the actual legislative work.
The verb used when a legislative body officially approves and passes a law. It implies a formal send-off from the drafting stage.
Parliament passed the new law.
This noun expresses thoughtful hesitation rather than outright refusal. It is diplomatic: you have considered and are worried, not simply opposed.
Coalition, parliamentary group, bill, Basic Law. The words come together like a puzzle. This is no longer a foreign language. This is a system I can understand.
The panel discussion is in full swing. Panelists debate with two-part connectors. Learn these powerful bracket constructions of German argumentation.
Master two-part connectors (sowohl...als auch, weder...noch)
Structure balanced arguments in German
Understand word order with paired conjunctions
The debate is alive. Four panelists at the table. One gestures passionately, another nods thoughtfully. The audience leans forward. Verumius writes in his notebook, capturing phrases.
On the one hand we must be open to new cultures. On the other hand we must not forget our own values.
I agree insofar as integration changes both society and the newcomers. It is not a one-way process.
In his notebook: 'Sowohl...als auch. Einerseits...andererseits. These words make the sentences smart.'
on the one hand

A: What do you think about the reform?
B: On the one hand it is necessary.
A: And on the other hand?
B: On the one hand yes, on the other hand too fast.
on the other hand

A: On the one hand the idea is good.
B: On the other hand it is expensive.
A: On the one hand...on the other. A dilemma.
B: Exactly. On the other hand there are always counterarguments.
however, yet

A: The plan sounds good, however there are risks.
B: What risks though?
A: The costs are high, yet manageable.
B: However, we must be careful.
on the contrary, in contrast

A: The government is in favor.
B: The opposition, in contrast, is against it.
A: And the citizens?
B: They, on the contrary, are divided.
both...and

A: Who is affected?
B: Both the government and the opposition.
A: Both locals and newcomers?
B: Yes, both the one group and the other.
neither...nor

A: Who agreed?
B: Neither the majority nor the minority.
A: Neither the government nor the opposition?
B: Exactly. Neither nor. Nobody.
admittedly...but

A: Is the proposal good?
B: It is admittedly good, but expensive.
A: Admittedly popular but unrealistic?
B: Admittedly not perfect, but a start.
the more...the more

A: The longer the debate, the clearer the viewpoints.
B: The more you discuss, the better you understand.
A: The more controversial, the more interesting?
B: Exactly. The more opinions, the richer the debate.
partly...partly

A: How were the reactions?
B: Partly positive, partly negative.
A: And the press?
B: Partly enthusiastic, partly critical.
not only...but also

A: Does he only speak German?
B: Not only German, but also Turkish.
A: Not only the government acts?
B: Not only the government, but also the citizens.
just as, equally as

A: Who does integration affect?
B: Newcomers just as much as locals.
A: Just as in other countries?
B: Yes, just as everywhere else.
the vote

A: When does the vote take place?
B: The vote is tomorrow.
A: How does the vote work?
B: By show of hands or roll-call vote.
the majority

A: Does the coalition have the majority?
B: Yes, a narrow majority.
A: Is a simple majority enough?
B: For this law we need a two-thirds majority.
the minority

A: Does the minority have rights?
B: Yes, minority protection is a fundamental principle.
A: Can the minority be outvoted?
B: Yes, but their rights remain protected.
the chancellor (male)

A: Who is the chancellor?
B: The chancellor leads the government.
A: What does the chancellor decide?
B: The chancellor determines the guidelines of policy.
the chancellor (female)

A: Who was the first female chancellor?
B: Angela Merkel was the first female chancellor.
A: How long was she chancellor?
B: She was chancellor for 16 years.
the constitution

A: Does Germany have a constitution?
B: Yes, the Basic Law is the constitution.
A: Can the constitution be changed?
B: Only with a two-thirds majority.
resistance

A: Is there resistance against the law?
B: Yes, strong resistance from the population.
A: How is the resistance expressed?
B: Through demonstrations and petitions.
to plead, to advocate

A: What do you advocate for?
B: I advocate for more dialogue.
A: Does the opposition advocate for a reform?
B: Yes, they advocate for fundamental changes.
to represent

A: Who do you represent?
B: I represent the citizens of my constituency.
A: Do you hold this viewpoint?
B: Yes, I represent it with conviction.

Two-part connectors frame your argument like brackets around the content.

Inclusion: sowohl...als auch (both)
Exclusion: weder...noch (neither)
Concession: zwar...aber (yes, but...)
Proportion: je...desto (proportional comparison)
Addition: nicht nur...sondern auch (addition with emphasis)

Key rule for je...desto: after 'je,' the verb goes to the end.
After 'desto,' the verb comes in position 2 (inversion).
These connectors are B2 tools for academic and professional discourse.

Sowohl...als auch: both elements get equal grammatical weight. 'Sowohl die Regierung als auch die Opposition hat/haben zugestimmt.' Singular or plural verb is debated, but plural is more common.

Weder...noch: double negation. 'Weder die Mehrheit noch die Minderheit war zufrieden.' Verb usually singular if both nouns are singular.

Je...desto: 'Je länger die Debatte dauert, desto klarer werden die Argumente.' The je-clause is a subordinate clause (verb at end). The desto-clause has verb-second (inversion).
The word 'sowohl' is the first half of a fixed pairing. It cannot stand alone. Look for the second element that closes the inclusive bracket.
The second bracket of the proportional comparison. Je mehr X, ... mehr Y.
The longer the debate lasts, the clearer the arguments become.
Concede a point, then deliver the counterpoint. The pattern: acknowledge, then push back.
On the one hand, on the other. Both, and also. Not only, but also. German sentences are brackets. You open them and must close them. Like mathematics, but with opinions.
Concessive clauses at B2 level. Learn the elevated variants of 'obwohl' and build the logical framework of formal argumentation.
Master concessive clauses: obgleich, wenngleich, wenn auch, selbst wenn
Distinguish between obwohl (B1) and its elevated variants
Build the logical framework of formal argumentation

A panelist raises her hand. 'Although we have made progress, it is not enough.' The moderator nods. 'Even though that is true, others see it differently.' Verumius underlines both words. Obgleich. Wenngleich. Same meaning, different weight.
although (literary)

A: Although the debate was heated, the tone remained respectful.
B: That is true. Although everyone had different opinions.
A: Although I was nervous, I spoke.
B: Well done, although it was difficult.
even though, albeit

A: Even though I respect your opinion, I do not share it.
B: That is fair. Even though we disagree.
A: Even though there is resistance, we must act.
B: Yes, even though the path is difficult.
even if, although

A: Even if not all agree, the resolution is valid.
B: Yes, even though the minority was against it.
A: Even if late, the reform came.
B: Better late than never, even if barely.
even if (strongest)

A: Even if the opposition votes against it, the coalition has the majority.
B: Even if everyone is against it?
A: Even if there is resistance, we must continue.
B: Yes, even if it gets difficult.
despite all that, nevertheless

A: The situation is difficult.
B: Despite all that, I believe in dialogue.
A: Optimistic despite all that?
B: Yes, despite all that. Always.
nevertheless, nonetheless

A: The proposal is contested.
B: Nevertheless we should examine it.
A: The criticism is justified, nevertheless...
B: Nevertheless we must act.
nonetheless, nevertheless

A: The concerns are justified.
B: Nonetheless we must act.
A: The risk is high, nonetheless...
B: Nonetheless the reform remains necessary.
so that, with the result that

A: The debate was constructive, so that a compromise became possible.
B: Everyone agreed, so that the law was passed.
A: He spoke clearly, so that everyone understood him.
B: Well formulated, so that no questions remained.
consequently, as a result

A: The vote failed.
B: Consequently the draft was revised.
A: The costs rose, as a result...
B: Consequently savings had to be made.
consequently, accordingly

A: The study shows clear results.
B: Accordingly the government must respond.
A: Consequently the reform was necessary?
B: Yes, accordingly there was no alternative.
thus, therefore

A: All arguments have been heard.
B: Thus the question is answered.
A: The majority agreed, thus...
B: Thus the law is decided.
the premise

A: What premise are you starting from?
B: My premise is that dialogue helps.
A: Is the premise correct?
B: If the premise is wrong, everything falls apart.
the conclusion, the inference

A: What is your conclusion?
B: My conclusion is clear: we need dialogue.
A: Is the conclusion logical?
B: The logical conclusion follows from the data.
the statement, the position paper

A: Is there an official statement?
B: Yes, the statement was published.
A: What does the statement say?
B: The statement demands more dialogue.
the conviction, the belief

A: What is your conviction?
B: In my firm conviction we need reforms.
A: Will you change your conviction?
B: No, that is a deep conviction.
willingness to compromise

A: Does the government show willingness to compromise?
B: Yes, the willingness to compromise is there.
A: Without willingness to compromise...
B: Without willingness to compromise every coalition fails.
to justify, to give reasons for

A: Can you justify your position?
B: Yes, I justify it with facts.
A: How do you justify that?
B: I justify it with three arguments.
to refute

A: Can the argument be refuted?
B: Yes, it can be easily refuted.
A: How does one refute a thesis?
B: By presenting counter-evidence.
to weigh up, to consider carefully

A: Have you weighed the pros and cons?
B: Yes, we weighed everything carefully.
A: What prevails when you weigh it up?
B: The advantages prevail, after we weighed it up.
to concede, to admit

A: Do you concede that he is right?
B: I concede that he has a point.
A: That is fair. You concede mistakes.
B: Yes, one must be able to concede mistakes.

Obwohl (B1) means 'although.' At B2 level, the following are added:
obgleich (literary 'although'), wenngleich (even though/albeit),
wenn auch (even if), selbst wenn (even if, strongest).

All are subordinating: verb goes to the end.
The adverbs (nichtsdestotrotz, trotz alledem) are NOT subordinating:
they stand in position 1 with the verb in position 2.

Example: 'Obgleich er dagegen war, stimmte er zu.'
vs. 'Er war dagegen, nichtsdestotrotz stimmte er zu.'
Same meaning, different grammar.

From weakest to strongest concession: obwohl (B1 standard) < obgleich (literary) < wenngleich (elevated, 'even though') < wenn auch (flexible) < selbst wenn (strongest, 'even if in the worst case'). The adverbs nichtsdestotrotz and nichtsdestoweniger are not subordinating conjunctions but connective adverbs. They take position 1 with verb inversion, unlike the subordinating conjunctions which send the verb to the end.
A subordinating conjunction sends the verb to the end of its clause. Check each option: only one correctly places the verb in both the subordinate and main clause.
A concessive conjunction meaning 'even though.' The elevated variant of obwohl. Verb goes to the end.
Even though I respect your opinion, I do not share it.
A two-part concessive structure: the first word signals a conceded point, the second introduces the contrasting counterargument.
Sowohl...als auch means both...and. Inclusion of both sides.
Obgleich, wenngleich, selbst wenn, nichtsdestotrotz. The language of concession. You grant your opponent a point and win anyway. The most elegant form of contradiction.
Verumius stands up and asks his question. In German. In front of everyone. Learn discourse markers that structure your arguments and the elegant technique of wenn-less conditionals.
Discourse markers for structured argumentation
